title: 3D‐Printed CuNi Self‐Supporting Electrocatalysts with Ordered Microchannels for Ammonia Production
content:
A 3D‐printed CuNi self‐supporting electrocatalyst features an ordered microchannel framework for enhanced mechanical integrity and rapid mass transport. The integration of superhydrophilic‐superaerophobic surfaces accelerates bubble release and electrolyte access, while synergistic CuNi bimetallic sites ensure highly selective, stable, and energy‐efficient production of ammonia and formate.









ABSTRACT
Self‐supporting catalysts have attracted massive research interest in electrocatalysis because of their excellent conductivity and high utilization of active sites. However, traditional substrates, e.g., metal foams and carbon paper, suffer from structural instability and slow mass transfer due to their irregular pore structure, leading to reduced catalytic activity. Herein, we employ selective laser melting technology to construct a three‐dimensional (3D) CuNi self‐supporting electrocatalyst with a microchannel array (CuNi‐P). The ordered and interconnected porous structure efficiently facilitates mass transfer, and the decorated nanoparticles provide additional active sites. The obtained CuNi‐P also exhibits outstanding mechanical properties, ensuring long‐term durability. Moreover, the surface of CuNi‐P exhibits superhydrophilic and superaerophobic properties, enabling efficient mass transfer across the solid‐liquid‐gas three‐phase interfaces. Cu and Ni act synergistically to enhance NO3− reduction to ammonia (NH3), delivering the lowest charge‐transfer resistance and a high NH3 yield rate of 10.6 mg h−1 cm−2 at −0.55 V. Additionally, CuNi‐P exhibits excellent electrocatalytic performance for glycerol oxidation reaction, enabling a bifunctional electrocatalytic system that couples NH3 and formate production. The system can achieve a high NH3 Faradaic efficiency of 95.5% at 1.6 V and a current density of 100 mA cm−2 at only 1.4 V, while maintaining stable performance for 500 h.

title: Laser Co‐Patterning Redefines Interface Engineering for High‐Efficiency and Durable Water Electrolysis
content:
A laser‐based integrated design strategy simultaneously patterns both electrode interfaces in proton exchange membrane water electrolyzers, establishing continuous transport pathways across the cell. This approach reduces operating voltage by 37% at 3 A cm−2 while improving durability tenfold. Multi‐scale simulations reveal that interfacial engineering promotes apparent kinetics and mass transport within the cell.








ABSTRACT
Proton exchange membrane water electrolysis is a critical pathway for hydrogen production, yet its efficiency and durability are constrained by the interfacial transport limitations within the cell. However, conventional approaches only focus on individual component or interface. Herein, we introduce an integrated design strategy that establishes the continuous transport pathway across the membrane electrode assembly by simultaneously laser patterning of gas diffusion layer (GDL)‐catalytic layer (CL) and proton exchange membrane (PEM)‐CL interface. Computational fluid dynamics and molecular dynamics simulations reveal the patterning on GDL promotes apparent kinetics and mass transport. Concurrently, laser patterning and subsequent acid treatment of the PEM enhance proton transport by facilitating the improved connectivity of water channels. Electrolytic cells assembled with the laser‐patterned GDL and PEM demonstrate an exceptional performance enhancement, achieving 1.90 V at 3 A/cm2—a 37.09% voltage reduction and 28.94% increase in electrolysis efficiency. Furthermore, the laser co‐patterned cells demonstrated a voltage decay rate of only 6.15 µV/h during a 1000‐hour durability test, an order of magnitude lower than unpatterned counterparts. This work establishes a novel design principle for the electrolyzer by creating an integrated mass transport pathway within the cell via laser patterning and demonstrates its transformative performance with a clear mechanistic insight.

title: Turning a battery material into a bioelectronic inhibitor
content:
Nature Reviews Materials, Published online: 13 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41578-026-00921-7

An article in Nature Materials presents a mineral-based bioelectronic approach that uses the battery material LiFePO4 to deliver highly localized, electronically controlled lithium for precise neural inhibition.

title: Viruses allegedly stolen from high-security lab cause stir in Brazil
content:
Nature, Published online: 13 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01211-6

The missing samples — reported to include chikungunya and dengue viruses — have been recovered, but questions linger over motive.

title: 14 things our PhD supervisors got right and why it mattered
content:
Nature, Published online: 13 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00853-w

PhD students reflect on how their supervisors made a meaningful difference — from quiet acts of kindness to career-shaping guidance.

title: The ‘crazy rule-defying’ genes that determine sex
content:
Nature, Published online: 13 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01101-x

A gripping account reveals the workings of the remarkable chromosomes that specify male or female development.

title: Human scientists trounce the best AI agents on complex tasks
content:
Nature, Published online: 13 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01199-z

State-of-the-industry report finds that, despite the limitations of artificial-intelligence systems, researchers have embraced them.

title: How I harness research to inform humanitarian relief efforts
content:
Nature, Published online: 13 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00197-5

As a social scientist at Médecins Sans Frontières, Beverley Stringer researches the many non-medical factors that affect health during emergencies.

title: Huge analysis of 320,000 careers suggests that productive researchers stay that way
content:
Nature, Published online: 13 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00744-0

Early publishing success, team size and international collaborations are some of the best predictors of future publication rates.

title: Unlocking the potential of the insulator–metal transition for photocatalysis
content:
Nature Energy, Published online: 13 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41560-026-02036-3

Designing photocatalysts typically demands complex engineering of junctions. Vanadium dioxide (VO2), however, can spontaneously form homogeneous junctions upon undergoing an insulator–metal transition. This transition in VO2 thin films under light increases the photocatalytic conversion rate of methane into hydrogen and light alkanes.

title: One-step-processed bilayer ethyl cellulose for full-colour sub-ambient daytime radiative cooling
content:
Nature Energy, Published online: 13 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41560-026-02039-0

Fabricating coloured radiative cooling coatings that are easy to produce and do not compromise cooling performance is challenging. Ying Liu et al. develop a one-step fabrication method that yields coloured ethyl cellulose coatings while preserving high solar reflectance.

title: An ultrasound-scanning in vivo light source
content:
Nature Materials, Published online: 13 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02556-z

A deep-tissue light source made from mechanoluminescent transducers stimulated by focused ultrasound enables wide imaging of live animal vasculature, and modulation of neuronal activity and behaviour.

title: Orbital-hybridization-induced Ising-type superconductivity in a confined gallium layer
content:
Nature Materials, Published online: 13 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02573-y

Orbital hybridization and quantum confinement are used to realize Ising-like superconductivity in trilayers of gallium, which alone is probably not strongly enough spin–orbit coupled to host such superconductivity under high in-plane magnetic fields.

title: All-van der Waals microcavities for low-loss nonlinear photonics
content:
Nature Materials, Published online: 13 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02574-x

Photonic components fabricated from van der Waals materials and patterned using focused ion beam lithography with aluminium passivation demonstrate quality factors exceeding 106 and an efficient nonlinear optical response.

title: Hydrophobic tuning with non-canonical amino acids in a copper metalloenzyme
content:
Nature Chemistry, Published online: 13 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41557-026-02116-7

Hydrophobicity plays an important role in protein function, but tuning hydrophobicity with canonical amino acids is chemically limited. Now through the genetic incorporation of bulky, highly hydrophobic non-canonical amino acids, their utility in enzyme engineering by enhancing the function of a copper-dependent laccase through hydrophobic tuning has been demonstrated.

title: Time-dependent surface polarization breaks static scaling relationship for selective acetylene hydrogenation
content:
Nature Chemistry, Published online: 13 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41557-026-02107-8

Scaling correlations, between adsorption energies of chemically related surface species, impose limits on selectivity in chemical processes, as exemplified by constraints in heterogeneous catalysis. Now it has been demonstrated that time-dependent surface polarization under oscillating potentials could overcome static scaling relationships and promote selectivity in acetylene semi-hydrogenation.

title: tRNA-deacylase-directed discovery of biosynthetic pathways
content:
Nature Chemistry, Published online: 13 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41557-026-02126-5

tRNA deacylases have evolved as resistance genes towards natural products that contain non-canonical amino acids by preventing their mistranslation. Now a strategy has been developed that leverages tRNA deacylases as class-agnostic genomic markers for amino acid-based biosynthetic gene clusters, identifying thousands of cryptic clusters and enabling the discovery of amino acid-based natural products.

title: Contrasting single-molecule magnet behaviour in dysprosium and terbium bis(stannolediide) complexes
content:
Nature Chemistry, Published online: 13 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41557-026-02114-9

Highly axial lanthanide complexes with strong magnetic anisotropy are attractive candidates for high-performance single-molecule magnets. Now dysprosium(III) and terbium(III) homoleptic bis(stannolediide) complexes have been synthesized, with the dysprosium compound exhibiting a high energy barrier to relaxation and a high blocking temperature. Access to a rare divalent Dy(II) analogue displaying magnetic anisotropy has also been demonstrated.
